rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Patricia Mcfaddens Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer To Margaret A. Mcfaddens Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Russell S. Roeca. Before the court is Patricia McFadden's Demurrer to Margaret A. McFadden's Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee; Request For Appointment Of California Licensed Professional Fiduciary To Serve As Temporary Trustee Pending Decision ("Demurrer"). The Demurrer to Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee; Request For Appointment Of California Licensed Professional Fiduciary To Serve As Temporary Trustee Pending Decision is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e), as follows. The court will briefly summarize the procedural history relevant to the Demurrer and the arguments presented. Margaret A. McFadden's Amendment To Amended Petition For Instructions Concerning Ascertaining The Beneficiaries And Trustee; Request For Appointment Of California Licensed Professional Fiduciary To Serve As Temporary Trustee Pending Decision ("Amendment to Amended Petition") was filed January 6, 2025, following this court's December 6, 2024 Order Granting In Part And Denying In Part Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication As To Trust Interpretation; Order Granting Leave To Amend. This filing amends the amended petition filed September 25, 2023. On December 7, 2023, Patricia McFadden filed a petition alleges that the Fourth Amendment and Addendum to the instant Trust are invalid because they were not executed by Trust Protector Michael Carroll and, alternatively, are invalid as products of Margaret's and Anne McFadden's undue influence over the settlor. In the December 6, 2024 Order on the motion for summary judgment or in the alternative motion for summary adjudication, the issue that survived summary adjudication was whether the Third Amendment to the Trust is the controlling instrument for the Survivor's Trust Created Under the McFadden 1996 Trust - as opposed to the Fourt Amendment and Addendum. As to this material issue, Margaret disputed whether compliance with the Third Amendment was impossible, impracticable, or excused. The court granted her oral request at the hearing for opportunity to amend the petition to adequately plead her position. The Amendment to Amended Petition . Per the Notice of Demurrer, Movant demurs to the Amendment to Amended Petition filed on January 6, 2025, at "Relief Requested - A [ B, and C]" on grounds that "The Second Amended Petition does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action." The statement that Patricia demurs to "Relief Requested - A" [B and C] appears to reference the Amended Petition's Amended Prayer for Relief section, which lists items under an A. B. C., etc. format. No other portion of the Amended Petition lists items this way.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] To survive a demurrer under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e), pleadings must contain "ultimate facts," rather than evidentiary facts or conclusions of law. The "ultimate facts" pleading standard requires factual particularity in pleading the essential elements of a legal claim, sufficient to acquaint a defendant with the nature, source and extent of the cause of action, but does not require the plaintiff to plead the specific evidence she will use to prove those elements. (Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531, 550.) The California Supreme Court in Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal. 4th 531, 550 established that a plaintiff may allege on information and belief any matters that are not within her personal knowledge, if she has information leading her to believe that the allegations are true. The MPA summarizes the noticed argument for demurrer to the Amendment to Amended Petition at 7:16-26, "In her SAP, Margaret does not allege that compliance with the exclusive means of modification specified in the Third Amendment was excused or impossible. She does not allege that Michael Carroll was dead or otherwise unavailable. She does not even allege that Michael Carroll acted improperly. Rather, Margaret merely alleges that Mary L. believed Michael Carroll would act in a neutral and disinterested manner regarding any future amendments, that Margaret believes that Michael Carroll was influenced to favor interests of family members other than Margaret, that he did not act in a neutral and disinterested manner, and that he refused to resign as trust protector. None of these allegations amount to impossibility, or a level of difficulty, expense or harm that equates to impossibility. [case citation.]" "Impossibility of performance" is a common law defense to an action for failure to perform an obligation, and has been codified in various California codes and discussed in various types of civil case law - as exampled by the parties' citations in the papers. Also, as exampled by parties' cited cases, there is no black letter test or series of elements established to determine "impossibility" in a given context or controversy, particularly in the probate trust context. As explained in the opposition to the demurrer, the Amended Petition in the "New Factual Allegations" section facially pleads on information and belief facts the petitioner alleges, collectively, made it impossible for the settlor to follow the Third Amendment's requirements for proper amendment of the Trust. The Amended Petition does not facially plead the information petitioner personally possesses that leads to her to make certain statements on information and belief. This is not sufficient factual pleading to withstand a demurrer on the basis of failure to state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e). However, the opposition to the Demurrer provides factual allegations that could be pleaded in the Amended Petition to address this and to more fulsomely plead the alleged "impossibility" of the settlor complying with the Third Amendment's requirements for trust amendment. The court must allow Margaret the opportunity to amend the Amendment to Amended Petition and cure these defects in pleading. Margaret McFadden shall file and serve a further amendment to the Amendment to Amended Petition filed January 6, 2025, within ten (10) days of service of Notice of Entry of Order. The amendment shall be pleaded so as to present the factual basis for pleadings made on information and belief and to plead facts going to the necessary elements of the "impossibility" theory advanced by Margaret McFadden in the filings and opposition to the Demurrer, and to amend the Amended Prayer for Relief, paragraphs A, B, and C, accordingly.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MARY L. MCFADDEN SURVIVOR'S TRUST, ESTABLISHED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] The Notice concludes, "The Second Amended Petition was amended following an order of summary adjudication permitting leave to amend. Because it fails to identify any triable issue of material fact for trial, summary judgment should be entered, or this demurrer should be granted without leave to amend." The MPA also states, at 5:10-11, "The Court should, therefore, grant Patricia's demurrer dismissing Margaret's petition without leave to amend or enter summary judgment in favor of Patricia." The repeated references to the Amended Petition's alleged lack of "disputed issue of material fact" and requests that the court entry summary judgment on Margaret's petition in favor of Movant is confusing, in the context of a demurrer. This hearing date was requested by Patricia and approved for hearing on a demurrer. The papers are noticed as a demurrer. The court will conduct analysis pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e), and not Code of Civil Procedure section 437c. The Reply argues new grounds for demurrer to the Amended Petition (see, e.g., 3:22-24). The court acknowledges the argument presented in the Reply that the Amendment to Amended Petition appears to be substantively a trust contest pleaded so as to avoid the consequences of a trust contest under the Trust's no contest clause and/or a statute of limitations. However, these grounds for demurrer presented for the first time in the Reply are not properly noticed and the court cannot consider them in ruling on the Demurrer now before it (see, Code of Civ. Proc. section 1010; Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1110(a)). Further, when considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.; Sheehan v. San Francisco 49ers, Ltd. (2009) Cal.4th 992, 998.). While the argument that the Amendment to Amended Petition is materially an untimely trust contest might find traction in a different procedural posture, a demurrer is not the vehicle by which the court can make such a determination and ruling. A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading, based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 996, 1008, 1009.) Demurrers test legal sufficiency based on the allegations as pleaded, without regard to the plaintiff's ability to prove the alleged facts. (see, Gray v. Dignity Health (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 225.) A ruling on a demurrer does not determine contested factual issues relating to the merits.

rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 4] Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
